title: Installing a greywater system for toilet flushing
subtitle: Recycling shower and bath water to cut mains water consumption for non-potable uses
excerpt: A typical UK household uses 30 to 40 litres of clean drinking water per person per day to flush toilets. Installing a greywater recycling system that takes treated shower and bath water instead reduces household mains water demand by 20 to 30 percent. Systems compliant with BS 8525-1:2010 are accepted by water companies and building inspectors for new builds and major refurbishments.
sourceNotes: BS 8525-1:2010 Greywater Systems for Domestic Applications. CIRIA C539 for greywater design guidance. WRAS Technical Guidance on greywater recycling. CIBSE Knowledge Series KS8 Water Efficiency in Buildings.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wastewater from showers, baths, and hand basins, as distinct from blackwater (toilet waste). Greywater contains soap, skin cells, and bacteria but no faecal matter. After basic treatment it is suitable for toilet flushing and garden irrigation but not for potable use.

The concentration of biodegradable organic material in wastewater, measured as BOD (biological oxygen demand) in mg/l. High BOD in stored greywater indicates active bacterial decomposition, causing odour and greywater quality deterioration. Systems must process greywater within 24 hours to prevent odour.

The sequence of processes a greywater system uses: typically settling and screening (to remove hair and debris), biological treatment or UV disinfection, and storage. BS 8525-1 defines the minimum treatment train required for toilet flushing use.

A device (check valve or air gap) that prevents greywater from contaminating the mains drinking water supply. BS 8525-1 requires the mains backup supply to enter the greywater storage tank through an AA-type air gap (25 mm minimum) to prevent siphonage of greywater into the mains.

content.text: Greywater

recycling is most cost-effective in households with ground-floor toilets immediately below first-floor bathrooms, where the pipework runs are short. Complex systems with long runs, pumping stages, or multiple treatment stages have higher capital and running costs that push payback beyond 15 years. The simplest compliant systems take the shower drain waste, pass it through a filter and UV unit, and pump it directly to the WC cistern.
In England and Wales, greywater recycling systems require the mains backup supply to meet the
backflow prevention

standard in BS 8525-1. Using greywater to flush toilets in a new build requires an approved document G compliance check. For an existing dwelling, a Part P notification may be needed if new electrical circuits are required for the pump or UV unit.
What you need

A purpose-designed greywater unit meeting BS 8525-1 with a settling chamber, coarse filter, UV disinfection lamp, and a centrifugal pump with float switch. Sized to the daily greywater input: a single shower of 50 litres needs a 50 to 100 litre tank.
50 mm solvent-weld waste pipe and fittings to connect the shower and bath outlets to the greywater unit inlet, typically through the floor or a wall.

15 mm copper or plastic pipe for the treated greywater supply to the WC cistern. The connection must include a dosing valve to prevent over-filling and a float valve at the cistern in place of or supplementing the standard mains float.
Greywater has a high
organic load

and will begin to smell within 24 to 48 hours if stored without treatment. Systems that simply collect and store greywater without biological or UV treatment are not compliant with BS 8525-1 and will cause severe odour in the toilet cistern within days. Only use systems with an active treatment stage.
System design considerations

Calculate the daily greywater supply. A 5-minute power shower uses 40 to 70 litres. A bath uses 80 to 150 litres. The system should be sized so the tank turns over completely every 24 hours to prevent stagnation.

Check the pipe routing. A gravity-fed system (shower drain above WC cistern) needs no pump. A pumped system (shower drain below or level with the WC) adds pump running cost and a potential failure point. In most UK houses, the first-floor bathroom is above the ground-floor toilet, making a gravity-fed system straightforward.

Plan the mains backup supply. On days when greywater supply is insufficient (holidays, low-occupancy periods), the WC cistern must receive a mains backup. The BS 8525-1 AA-type air gap must be included in this backup connection.
The treatment train
The
treatment train

in a compliant unit starts with a coarse filter to remove hair and soap, then settles in a tank so heavier particles sink, then passes through a UV lamp to kill bacteria, before being pumped to the WC cistern. Replace the UV lamp once a year (20 to 30 pounds). Clean the coarse filter monthly.
What done well looks like

The WC cistern flushes with treated greywater on 300 or more days per year. Mains backup operates automatically when greywater supply is insufficient. No odour from the WC cistern. Water meter readings confirm a 20 to 25 percent reduction in mains consumption after installation. UV lamp is replaced annually on schedule.
What done badly looks like

A non-compliant simple storage tank was installed without UV treatment. Within a week the WC smells of stale drains. The tank was undersized for the household size and runs dry by midday on weekdays, causing the mains backup to compensate for most flushes. The net water saving is under 5 percent because the system is in mains backup mode most of the time.
Troubleshooter

attrs.cause: UV lamp has failed or is at end of life, allowing bacteria to multiply in the stored greywater
attrs.fix: Replace the UV lamp. Check the lamp indicator light: most units have a lamp-failure indicator. After replacement, run the cistern through three complete flush cycles to clear the old water.

attrs.symptom: WC cistern takes longer than usual to fill
attrs.cause: Coarse filter in the greywater unit is partially blocked, reducing flow to the pump
attrs.fix: Remove and clean the coarse filter. Under running water, back-flush the mesh to remove accumulated hair and soap. Clean monthly as part of routine maintenance.

attrs.symptom: Greywater unit alarm is sounding continuously
attrs.cause: Tank has reached high-level alarm: either the float switch is faulty or the outlet to the WC is blocked
attrs.fix: Check that the outlet to the WC is not kinked or blocked. If the pump runs but the level does not drop, the pump outlet may be airlocked. Run the system through a manual cycle and check the pump is delivering flow at the WC.
